volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.2.104)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Sequere hac me , gnata , ut munus fungaris tuom .
sections.translation: Follow me this way , daughter , that you may perform your office .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.2
sections.original: Sequor , sed finem fore quem dicam nescio .
sections.translation: I am following , but I know not what to say will be the end of our journey .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.3
sections.original: Adest . em illaé sunt aedes , i intro nunciam .—
sections.translation: ’ Tis here . See , this is the house . Now go you in .( ( Exit POVERTY , who enters the house of CHARMIDES .) )

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.4
sections.original: nunc , ne quis erret vostrum , paucis in viam
sections.translation: ( to the AUDIENCE .) Now , that no one of you may be mistaken , in a few words

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: deducam , si quidem operam dare promittitis .
sections.translation: I will conduct you into the right path , if , indeed , you promise to listen to me . First , then , I will now tell you who I am , and who she is who has gone in here ( pointing to the house ) , if you give your attention . In the first place , Plautus has given me the name of Luxury , and then he has willed that this Poverty should be my daughter .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: sed ea huc quid introierit impulsu meo
sections.translation: But why , at my suggestion , she has just entered here , listen and give attentive ear while I inform you . There is a certain young man who is living in this house ; by my assistance he has squandered away his paternal estate . Since I see that there is nothing left for him to support me ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: dedi ei meam gnatam , quicum aétatem exigat .
sections.translation: I have given him my daughter , together with whom to pass his life . But expect nothing about the plot of this play : the old men who will come hither will disclose the matter to you . The name of this play in the Greek is The Treasure [ Thesaurus ] ; Philemon wrote it [*] : Plautus translated it into Latin [*] ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: nomen Trinummo fecit , nunc hoc vos rogat
sections.translation: and gave it the name of The Three Pieces of Money [ Trinummus ] . Now , he begs this of you , that it may be allowed the play to keep that name . Thus much have I to say . Farewell . Attend in silence .

sections.label: Section 1.1.23
sections.original: Amicum castigare ob meritam noxiam
sections.translation: To reprove one ’ s friend for a fault that deserves it , is a thankless task ; but sometimes ’ tis useful

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: et conducibile . nám ego amicum hodie meum
sections.translation: and ’ tis profitable . Therefore , this day will I soundly reprove my friend for a fault that much deserves it . Unwilling am I , did not my friendship bid me do it . For this faultiness has encroached too much upon good morals , so drooping now are nearly all of them .

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.original: sed dum illi aegrotant , interim mores mali
sections.translation: But while they are in this distempered state , bad morals , in the mean time , have sprung up most plenteously , like well-watered plants ; nor is there now anything abundant here but these same bad morals . Of them you may now reap a most plenteous harvest :

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: faciunt pars hominum quam id quod prosint pluribus .
sections.translation: and here a set of men are making the favour of a few of much more value than that in which they may benefit the many . Thus private interests outdo that which is to the public advantage — interests which in many points are a hindrance , and a nuisance , and cause an obstruction both to private and to public welfare .

sections.label: Section 1.2.39
sections.original: Larem corona nostrum decorari volo .
sections.translation: I wish our household God [*] to be graced with a chaplet .

sections.label: Section 1.2.40
sections.original: uxor , venerare ut nobis haec habitatio
sections.translation: Wife [*] ( addressing her within ) , pay him due respect , that this dwelling may turn out for us prosperous , lucky , happy , and fortunate ; and ( in a lower voice ) that , as soon as I possibly may , I may see you dead and gone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.43
sections.original: Hic ille ést , senecta aetate qui factust puer ,
sections.translation: This is he who in his old age has become a child [*] — who has been guilty of a fault that deserves correction .

sections.label: Section 1.2.45
sections.original: adgrediar hominem .
sections.translation: I will accost the man .

sections.label: Section 1.2.45b
sections.original: Quoia hic vox prope me sonat ?
sections.translation: Whose voice is it that sounds near me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.46
sections.original: Tui benevolentis , sí ita es ut ego te volo ,
sections.translation: Of one who wishes you well , if you are as I desire you to be ; but , if you are otherwise , of one who is your enemy , and is angry with you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.48
sections.original: O amice , salve , átque aequalis . ut vales ,
sections.translation: Health to you , O my friend and years ’- mate ! How are you , Megaronides ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.49b
sections.original: Et tu edepol salve , Callicles .
sections.translation: And , i ’ faith [*] health to you , Callicles !

sections.label: Section 1.2.50
sections.original: Valen ? valuistin ?
sections.translation: Are you well ? Have you been well ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.50b
sections.original: Valeo , et valui rectius .
sections.translation: I am well , and I have been still better .

sections.label: Section 1.2.51
sections.original: Quid tua agit uxor ? ut valet ?
sections.translation: And how does your wife do ? How is she ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.51b
sections.original: Plus quam ego volo .
sections.translation: Better than I wish .

sections.label: Section 1.2.52
sections.original: Bene hercle est illam tibi valere et vivere .
sections.translation: ’ Tis well , i ’ faith , for you , that she is alive and well

sections.label: Section 1.2.53
sections.original: Credo hercle te gaudere , si quid mihi mali est .
sections.translation: Troth , I believe that you are glad if I have any misfortune .

sections.label: Section 1.2.54
sections.original: Omnibus amicis quod mihi est cupio esse idem .
sections.translation: That which I have , I wish for all my friends as well .

sections.label: Section 1.2.55
sections.original: Eho tu , tua uxor quid agit ?
sections.translation: Harkye , how does your wife do ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.55b
sections.original: Immortalis est ,
sections.translation: She is immortal ; she lives , and is likely to live .

sections.label: Section 1.2.56b
sections.original: Bene hercle nuntias ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you tell me good news ; and I pray the Gods that , surviving you , she may last out your life .

sections.label: Section 1.2.58
sections.original: Dum quidem hercle tecum nupta sit , sane velim .
sections.translation: By my troth ! if indeed she were only married to yourself , I could wish it sincerely .

sections.label: Section 1.2.59
sections.original: Vin commutemus , tuam ego ducam et tu meam ?
sections.translation: Do you wish that we should exchange ?— that I should take yours , and you mine ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.60
sections.original: faxo haud tantillum dederis verborum mihi .
sections.translation: I ’ d be making you not to get a bit the better of the bargain of me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.61
sections.original: Namque enim tu , credo , me imprudentem obrepseris .
sections.translation: Indeed , I fancy [*] you would not be surprising me unawares .

sections.label: Section 1.2.62
sections.original: Ne tu hercle faxo haud nescias quam rem egeris .
sections.translation: Aye , faith , I should cause you not to be knowing [*] the thing you were about .

sections.label: Section 1.2.63
sections.original: Habeas ut nanctu'snanctus es : nota mala res optumast .
sections.translation: Keep what you ’ ve got ; the evil that we know is the best . But if I were now to take one that I know not , I should not know what to do .

sections.label: Section 1.2.65
sections.original: Edepol proinde ut diu vivitur , bene vivitur .
sections.translation: In good sooth , just as one lives [*] a long life , one lives a happy life .

sections.label: Section 1.2.66
sections.original: sed hoc ánimum advorte atque aufer ridicularia ;
sections.translation: But give your attention to this , and have done with your joking , for I am come hither to you for a given purpose .

sections.label: Section 1.2.67b
sections.original: Quid venis ?
sections.translation: Why have you come ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.68
sections.original: Malis te ut verbis multis multum obiurigem .
sections.translation: That I may rebuke you soundly with many harsh words .

sections.label: Section 1.2.69
sections.original: Men ?
sections.translation: Me , do you say ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.69b
sections.original: Numquis est hic alius praeter me atque te ?
sections.translation: Is there any one else here besides you and me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.70
sections.original: Nemost .
sections.translation: There is no one .

sections.label: Section 1.2.70b
sections.original: Quid tu igitur rogitas , tene obiurigem ?
sections.translation: Why , then , do you ask if ’ tis you I mean to rebuke ? Unless , indeed , you think that I am about to reprove my own self . For if your former principles now flag in you , or if the manners of the age are working a change in your disposition , and if you preserve not those of the olden time , but are catching up these new ones ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.75
sections.original: omnibus amicis morbum tu incuties gravem ,
sections.translation: you will strike all your friends with a malady so direful , that they will turn sick at seeing and hearing you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.77
sections.original: Qui in mentem venit tibi istaec dicta dicere ?
sections.translation: How comes it into your mind to utter these expressions ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.78
sections.original: Quia omnís bonos bonasque adcurare addecet ,
sections.translation: Because it becomes all good men and all good women to have a care to keep suspicion and guilt away from themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.80
sections.original: Non potest utrumque fieri .
sections.translation: Both cannot be done .

sections.label: Section 1.2.80b
sections.original: Quapropter ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.80c
sections.original: Rogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask ? I am the keeper of my own heart so as not to admit guilt there ; suspicion is centred in the heart of another . For if now I should suspect that you had stolen the crown from the head of Jupiter in the Capitol [*] ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.85
sections.original: qui in columine astat summo : si id non feceris
sections.translation: the statue which stands on the highest summit of the temple ; if you had not done so , and still it should please me to suspect you , how could you prevent me from suspecting you ? But I am anxious to know what this matter is .

sections.label: Section 1.2.89
sections.original: Haben tu amicum aut familiarem quempiam ,
sections.translation: Have you any friend or intimate acquaintance

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: quoi pectus sapiat ?
sections.translation: whose judgment is correct ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.90b
sections.original: Edepol haud dicam dolo :
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ ll tell you without reserve . There are some whom I know to be friends ; there are some whom I suspect to be so , but whose dispositions and feelings I am unable to discover , whether they incline to the side of a friend or an enemy ; but of my assured friends , you are the most assured .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: si quid scis me fecisse inscite aut improbe ,
sections.translation: If you know that I have done anything unwittingly or wrongfully , and if you do not accuse me of it , then you yourself will be to blame .

sections.label: Section 1.2.96b
sections.original: Scio ;
sections.translation: I know it ; and if I had come hither to you for any other purpose , you request what is right .

sections.label: Section 1.2.98
sections.original: Expecto si quid dicas .
sections.translation: If you have anything to say , I am waiting for it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.98b
sections.original: Primumdum omnium ,
sections.translation: Then , first of all , you are badly spoken of in general conversation by the public .

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: turpilucricupidum te vocant cives tui ;
sections.translation: Your fellow-citizens are calling you greedy of grovelling gain [*] ; and then , again , there are others who nickname you a vulture [*] , and say that you care but little whether you devour enemies or fellow-citizens . Since I have heard these things said against you , I have , to my misery , been sadly agitated .

sections.label: Section 1.2.104
sections.original: Est atque non est mi in manu , Megaronides :
sections.translation: It is , and it is not , in my power , Megaronides :
